article_title: I Replaced Microsoft 365 With This Free Program, and I'm Happy With the Switch
core_thesis: The article argues that LibreOffice, a free and open-source office suite, is a viable alternative to Microsoft 365 for users who prioritize cost savings, privacy, and basic functionality over cloud collaboration and mobile access. The author shares their personal experience switching from Microsoft 365 to LibreOffice, highlighting both the benefits (free, private, customizable) and drawbacks (no cloud, no mobile app, limited support).
detailed_summary: The author, Zachary McAuliffe, explains his decision to replace Microsoft 365 with LibreOffice primarily to save money, but also to avoid Microsoft's AI assistant Copilot. LibreOffice is a free, open-source suite maintained by The Document Foundation, with roots dating back to 1985. It includes six applications: Writer (Word), Calc (Excel), Impress (PowerPoint), Draw (Visio), Base (Access), and Math (formula editor). It runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and offers a portable version for USB drives. Key advantages include no data collection, local file storage with encryption options (GNU Privacy Guard), and compatibility with many file types (though complex documents may have formatting issues). Disadvantages include a cluttered interface, no native cloud integration, no mobile app (except an experimental Android reader), and no official customer support. The author concludes that LibreOffice is ideal for users who need basic offline productivity and value privacy, but not for those who require real-time collaboration or mobile editing.

key_data_points: LibreOffice is completely free and open-source, with no subscription fees, making it a cost-saving alternative to Microsoft 365 (🟢 relevant to your student budget: saves money for other tools or subscriptions).

LibreOffice does not collect user data, create user profiles, or track document interactions, offering greater privacy than Microsoft 365 which collects hardware/software data and IP addresses (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity interest: aligns with privacy-first practices and can be used for sensitive documents).

LibreOffice supports local file encryption using GNU Privacy Guard (GPG) keys, adding a layer of security for stored documents (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: understanding GPG encryption can be useful for secure communication or data protection in operations).

LibreOffice is compatible with Microsoft Word, Excel, and Apple Pages files, but complex documents exchanged multiple times between suites may experience formatting issues (🟢 relevant to programming: you may need to handle file format conversions in scripts or automation).

LibreOffice offers a portable version that runs from a USB drive without installation, allowing use on any Windows, macOS, or Linux computer (🟢 relevant to homelabbing: useful for creating a portable toolkit for system administration or testing).

LibreOffice has no native cloud integration or real-time collaboration features; documents must be manually synced via cloud storage or USB (🟢 relevant to DevOps: you might integrate LibreOffice with cloud storage APIs for automated workflows).

The Document Foundation does not provide individual technical support; users rely on community forums and bug reports (🟢 relevant to your self-sufficiency: you can leverage open-source community for troubleshooting).
methodology_steps: Download LibreOffice from the official website (https://www.libreoffice.org) for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux).
Install the suite using the standard installer; no account creation or subscription is required.
Launch any application (e.g., Writer for word processing, Calc for spreadsheets) from the start menu or applications folder.
To open a Microsoft 365 document, use File > Open and select the file (e.g., .docx, .xlsx). LibreOffice will attempt to convert it; save in LibreOffice format (.odt, .ods) for full compatibility.
To encrypt a document, go to File > Save As, check 'Save with password', and set a password. For GPG encryption, use File > Digital Signatures or external GPG tools.
To use the portable version, download the portable package from the LibreOffice website, extract it to a USB drive, and run the executable directly from the drive on any compatible computer.
To customize LibreOffice, visit the Extension Center (https://extensions.libreoffice.org) to download and install extensions (e.g., citation templates, spellcheckers).
conclusions: LibreOffice is a strong free alternative to Microsoft 365 for users who need basic offline productivity and value privacy, as it collects no user data and allows local encryption. The author successfully replaced Microsoft 365 with LibreOffice for personal budgeting and writing, saving money without sacrificing essential features.

The main trade-offs are the lack of cloud integration, real-time collaboration, and mobile support, which may be deal-breakers for users who work across multiple devices or need to collaborate remotely. For those who can work around these limitations (e.g., using a USB drive or cloud storage for syncing), LibreOffice offers a cost-effective and privacy-respecting solution.

LibreOffice's open-source nature allows advanced users to inspect and modify the code, which can be appealing for cybersecurity enthusiasts or developers who want to audit software for vulnerabilities or customize functionality.
personal_relevance_summary: As a student and programmer, this article is relevant because it introduces a free, privacy-focused office suite that can save you money while offering basic productivity tools. For your cybersecurity interests, LibreOffice's open-source code and local encryption options provide a secure environment for handling sensitive documents, and you can audit the code for vulnerabilities. For homelabbing and DevOps, the portable version is a handy tool for system administration tasks on multiple machines. The article also highlights the trade-offs of using open-source software, which aligns with your technical curiosity and self-sufficiency mindset.
extra_advice: Consider integrating LibreOffice with your homelab by automating document conversion or encryption tasks using its command-line interface (e.g., `libreoffice --headless --convert-to pdf`). This can be useful for generating reports or processing documents in scripts. Additionally, explore the GPG encryption feature to practice secure file handling, which is a valuable skill for Red Teaming operations involving data exfiltration or secure communication.
